title: Quiz: Quantum Graph Coloring Algorithms
description: Test your understanding of quantum approaches to graph coloring

questions.question: The chromatic number chi(G) of a graph G is:

options.text: The number of vertices in G
The minimum number of colors needed to properly color G
The maximum degree of any vertex in G
The number of edges in G

questions.explanation: The chromatic number chi(G) is the minimum k such that G has a valid k-coloring - a coloring where no two adjacent vertices have the same color. For example, chi(K_n) = n for complete graphs, while chi(tree) = 2 for any tree.

questions.question: In the QUBO formulation for k-coloring, what do the binary variables x_{v,c} represent?

options.text: Whether edge (v,c) exists in the graph
Whether vertex v has color c
The weight of edge from v to c
Whether v and c are adjacent

questions.explanation: In the QUBO encoding, x_{v,c} = 1 indicates that vertex v is assigned color c. The constraint sum_c x_{v,c} = 1 ensures each vertex gets exactly one color, encoded via penalty terms in the QUBO objective.

questions.question: The QUBO penalty term B * sum_{(u,v) in E} sum_c x_{u,c} * x_{v,c} enforces:

options.text: Each vertex has exactly one color
All vertices have the same color
The number of colors is minimized

questions.explanation: This penalty term adds cost B whenever two adjacent vertices (u,v) share the same color c (both x_{u,c} = x_{v,c} = 1). Minimizing this term drives the solution toward valid colorings where adjacent vertices differ.

questions.question: For Grover search applied to k-coloring with n vertices, the search space size is:
questions.explanation: Each of the n vertices can be assigned any of k colors independently, giving k^n total possible colorings. Grover search can find a valid coloring (if one exists) in O(sqrt(k^n / M)) queries, where M is the number of valid colorings.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the XY mixer in constrained QAOA for graph coloring?

options.text: To apply X and Y gates randomly
To preserve the one-hot constraint during evolution
To measure in the X and Y bases

questions.explanation: The XY mixer generates transitions between one-hot encoded states (exactly one qubit set per vertex) while preserving the total Hamming weight. This keeps the state within the valid subspace where each vertex has exactly one color assigned.

questions.question: Determining whether a graph is 3-colorable is NP-complete, but determining whether it is 2-colorable (bipartite) is in P.
questions.explanation: True. Testing bipartiteness (2-colorability) can be done in linear time using BFS/DFS to check for odd cycles. However, 3-colorability is NP-complete - one of the classic NP-complete problems. The complexity jumps dramatically from k=2 to k=3.

questions.question: For a planar graph (like a map), the Four Color Theorem guarantees:
questions.explanation: The Four Color Theorem proves that any planar graph can be colored with at most 4 colors. Furthermore, a 4-coloring can be found in linear time. This means quantum algorithms provide no asymptotic advantage for planar graph 4-coloring.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid applications of graph coloring? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Register allocation (a) colors interference graphs, scheduling (b) colors conflict graphs, and frequency assignment (c) colors interference graphs to avoid signal conflicts. Shortest path (d) is a different graph problem not related to coloring.
